mappings.rationaleAI: 8th-century poetry covers Nara literary flowering (B.3.a). Tale of Genji represents Heian literary culture (B.3.b.i). Zen influence connects to Kamakura culture (C.1.d). Muromachi period connects to C.2. 15th-16th century cultural development addresses C.3.c. Later literary traditions address C.4.c.

Warrior class spans feudal Japan (C). Rise of samurai addresses Heian decline (B.3.b.ii). Bakufu founding addresses Kamakura (C.1, C.1.a). Daimyo rise addresses C.1.e. Muromachi addresses C.2. Sengoku unification addresses C.3.a. Azuchi-Momoyama connects to C.3.d. Tokugawa addresses C.4, C.4.b.
Meiji Restoration addresses bakuhan collapse (C.4.d). Western opening addresses economic crises (C.4.d.i). Western pressure addresses Tempo reforms (C.4.d.iii).
Confucian influence connects to Kamakura Neo-Confucianism (C.1.d). Tokugawa Confucianism addresses C.4.d.ii. Korean Confucian state addresses D.5.a.
Buddhism's introduction to Japan (B.2.a, B.2.b). Nara Buddhist flowering (B.3.a). Kamakura Buddhist culture (C.1.d). Zen influence on arts (C.3.c). Korean Buddhism (D.2).

Korean history from ancient times addresses Korea to 1910 (D) spanning prehistoric origins (D.1), Three Kingdoms (D.2), Silla (D.3), Koryo (D.4), Yi dynasty (D.5, D.5.a, D.5.b). Introduction connects to A.
Mongol invasions address resistance to invasions of Japan (C.1.c). Koryo experience addresses D.4.
Dynastic rule provides framework for Japanese imperial/shogunal succession (B.2, C) and Korean dynasties (D).
Archaeological methods connect to historical sources (A), Japanese prehistory (B.1), and Korean prehistoric origins (D.1).
Esoteric Buddhist traditions provide context for Kamakura Buddhist culture (C.1.d).
Southeast Asian civilizations provide regional context for Japanese and Korean civilizations (A).
Catholic Jesuit missionary activity provides context for Portuguese and Spanish arrival in Japan (C.3.b).
